informal_statement.text: Given a binary search tree whose in-order traversal is strictly ascending and two keys both present in the tree, return the key of the lowest node that has both keys in its subtree.
preconditions: the input tree is a valid BST
both query keys are present in the tree
required_properties: the returned key appears in the tree
both query keys are in the subtree rooted at the returned key
no proper descendant of the returned key has both query keys in its subtree
edge_cases: both keys equal (returned key equals them)
one key is an ancestor of the other
keys on opposite sides of the root
tree with only a single path

semantic_units.description: BST precondition: the input tree is a valid BST.
common_failure_modes: omitting the BST precondition and solving general-tree LCA
assuming strict ordering within equal keys

semantic_units.description: Presence precondition: both query keys are present in the tree.
common_failure_modes: returning None silently when a key is missing

semantic_units.description: Ancestor property: both query keys lie in the subtree rooted at the returned key.
common_failure_modes: returning any common ancestor instead of the lowest one

semantic_units.description: Lowest: no proper descendant of the returned node contains both keys.
common_failure_modes: using LCA of general trees (parent-pointer style) semantics

semantic_units.description: Key equality case: when p == q, the LCA is that same key.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p q a : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → HasKey t q → lcaBst t p q = some a → ∃ sub : Tree, IsSubtree sub t ∧ (match sub with | Tree.nil => False | Tree.node _ k _ => k = a) ∧ HasKey sub p ∧ HasKey sub q
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing both query keys, the returned key is the root of a subtree containing both queries.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p q a : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → HasKey t q → lcaBst t p q = some a → ∀ (sub : Tree), IsProperSubtree sub t → (match sub with | Tree.nil => True | Tree.node _ k _ => k = a → False) → HasKey sub p → HasKey sub q → False
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing both query keys, no proper subtree that is not rooted at the returned key contains both queries and sits strictly below the LCA.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → lcaBst t p p = some p
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing the key, the LCA of a key with itself is that key.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def InOrder (t : BST α) : List α := ...
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Define the in-order traversal of the BST as a list of keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def Subtree (t : BST α) (x : α) : Prop := x ∈ keys(t) ∧ (x is in the subtree rooted at some node of t)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Define what it means for a key to be in a subtree (or equivalently, define the set of keys contained in a subtree).

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def LowestBoth (t : BST α) (k1 k2 : α) : Prop := ∃ n, n is a node of t ∧ k1 ∈ keys(subtree(n)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(n)) ∧ ∀ m, m is a node on the path from root to n (excluding n) → ¬(k1 ∈ keys(subtree(m)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(m)))
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Define the specification: the lowest node whose subtree contains both keys (i.e., closest to leaves among nodes containing both).

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def LCAKey (t : BST α) (k1 k2 : α) : α := ...
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Optionally define the key returned by the algorithm as the key stored at the lowest node whose subtree contains both keys (often equivalent to the LCA in a BST when both keys are present).

generated_obligations.lean_statement: (∀ x ∈ InOrder t, True) ∧ StrictAsc (InOrder t)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Assume the BST is well-formed in the sense that its in-order traversal is strictly ascending.
Assume both keys are present in the tree.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def PathTo (t : BST α) (k : α) : List (Node α) := ...
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Define the unique search path from the root to the node containing key k in a BST.
From strict ascending in-order traversal, derive the BST ordering property (left subtree keys < node key < right subtree keys).

generated_obligations.lean_statement: BSTProperty t → ∀ k ∈ keys(t), PathTo t k ends at the unique node whose key is k
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: In a BST, searching for a present key reaches exactly the node containing that key.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: BSTProperty t → ∀ n, keys(subtree(left(n))) ⊆ {x | x < key(n)} ∧ keys(subtree(right(n))) ⊆ {x | x > key(n)}
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: State the ordering constraints on keys in left/right subtrees for any node.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: BSTProperty t → ∀ n, (k1 ∈ keys(subtree(n)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(n))) ↔ (n lies on both PathTo t k1 and PathTo t k2)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Characterize nodes whose subtrees contain both keys as exactly the common ancestors (or equivalently nodes on both search paths).

generated_obligations.lean_statement: BSTProperty t → ∃! n, n is a node of t ∧ k1 ∈ keys(subtree(n)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(n)) ∧ ∀ m on path root→n excluding n, ¬(k1 ∈ keys(subtree(m)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(m)))
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Prove existence and uniqueness of the lowest node whose subtree contains both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: let r := rust_reference t k1 k2; LowestBoth t k1 k2 ∧ r = key(n) for the unique n satisfying LowestBoth t k1 k2
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Main correctness: the implementation returns the key stored at the unique lowest node whose subtree contains both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: let r := rust_reference t k1 k2; (k1 ∈ keys(t) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(t) ∧ StrictAsc (InOrder t)) → (∃ n, n is node of t ∧ key(n)=r ∧ k1 ∈ keys(subtree(n)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(n)))
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Soundness: the returned key corresponds to a node whose subtree contains both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: let r := rust_reference t k1 k2; (k1 ∈ keys(t) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(t) ∧ StrictAsc (InOrder t)) → ∀ m, m is node on path root→node(r) excluding node(r) → ¬(k1 ∈ keys(subtree(m)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(m)))
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Minimality: no proper ancestor of the returned node has both keys in its subtree.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: rust_reference t k1 k2 terminates for all finite BSTs t
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Prove the Rust implementation terminates (e.g., recursion/loop decreases along the search path).

generated_obligations.lean_statement: (k1 ∈ keys(t) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(t)) → ∃ r, rust_reference t k1 k2 = r
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: If both keys are present, the function returns some key (no failure/None case).

generated_obligations.lean_statement: StrictAsc (InOrder t) → (k1 ≠ k2 → returned key is well-defined and unique)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: If keys are distinct, the returned key is uniquely determined by the lowest-both-node property.
